coredata.dc:title: High quantum-yield carbon dots embedded metal-organic frameworks for selective and sensitive detection of dopamine
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Siqi
Li, Xujie
Wang, Lumin
Zhu, Fawei
Zhao, Xinyi
Yuan, Tianqi
Liu, Qi
Chen, Xiaoqing
coredata.dc:description: By embedding high quantum yield blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8), the luminescent metal-organic frameworks (LMOFs) - CDs@ZIF-8 composites were fabricated facilely and served as the fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of dopamine (DA). In which the sustainable pores within ZIF-8 would not only provide a natural habitat for analyte but also can selectively capture and concentrate the DA molecules via the interaction between the analyte DA and the basic site of the frame. Meanwhile, the CDs can be served as the signal probes to transform chemical signals of the interaction between CDs and analytes into fluorescent signals. Thus, the as-synthesized CDs@ZIF-8 composites generate new sensing performance when compared with CDs. As a consequence, a wide concentration dynamic range of 0.1–200 μM with a low detection limit (LOD) 16.64 nM was favorably obtained for the CDs@ZIF-8 composites based detection method for DA. Besides, the practicality and viability of the fluorescent composites were also successfully verified via challenging the CDs@ZIF-8 based sensing method to detect DA in complicated serum and urine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Luminescent metal-organic framework
Carbon dots
Dopamine detection
ZIF-8
Fluorescent probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application in human serum and urine

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21878339 , 21904141 and 22078369 ), the Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 2019JJ50791), the Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Safety Monitoring and Early Warning (No. 2020KFJJ06), and the Key R&D Project in the industrial field funded by Hunan Provincial Science &Technology Department, China (2019GK2131).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis of CDs@ZIF-8 and its application in DA sensing.

Photographs of CDs@ZIF-8 suspended in DA solution (A) before and (B) after centrifugation under natural light, and the photographs of CDs@ZIF-8 dispersion in the (C) absence and (D) presence of DA under 365 nm UV light.
(A) Possible mechanism for the detection of DA; (B) The fluorescence decay curves of CDs@ZIF-8 in the absence and presence of DA; (C) TEM image of CDs@ZIF-8/DA-quinone.
(A) FL emission spectra of the CDs@ZIF-8 based sensor exposed to various concentrations of DA. (B) The non-linear curve of the FL intensity versus the concentration of DA.
Comparison of proposed method with others in terms of response range and LOD.
Determination of DA in human serum and urine.

head.ce:title: High quantum-yield carbon dots embedded metal-organic frameworks for selective and sensitive detection of dopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Water Environment and Agriculture Product Safety, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Water Environment and Agriculture Product Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Water Environment and Agriculture Product Safety, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: By embedding high quantum yield blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8), the luminescent metal-organic frameworks (LMOFs) - CDs@ZIF-8 composites were fabricated facilely and served as the fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of dopamine (DA). In which the sustainable pores within ZIF-8 would not only provide a natural habitat for analyte but also can selectively capture and concentrate the DA molecules via the interaction between the analyte DA and the basic site of the frame. Meanwhile, the CDs can be served as the signal probes to transform chemical signals of the interaction between CDs and analytes into fluorescent signals. Thus, the as-synthesized CDs@ZIF-8 composites generate new sensing performance when compared with CDs. As a consequence, a wide concentration dynamic range of 0.1–200 μM with a low detection limit (LOD) 16.64 nM was favorably obtained for the CDs@ZIF-8 composites based detection method for DA. Besides, the practicality and viability of the fluorescent composites were also successfully verified via challenging the CDs@ZIF-8 based sensing method to detect DA in complicated serum and urine.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are one of fascinating material classes for preparing functional composites due to their adjustable surface function, permanent porosity and hybrid structural composition [1], and by combining specific metal nodes with functional organic ligands, unique functional MOFs can also be specially designed. The diversified physicochemical properties, tunable size and structural tunability of MOFs make them widely applied in gas storage and separation [2,3], catalysis [4–6], chemical and physical sensing [7–11], medical delivery [12,13] as well as ion-exchange [14,15]. In particular, the metal nodes with unsaturated coordination, different basic/acidic functions and luminescent structural units can serve as functional sites for selective sensing [16–18].

As an important sub-category of MOFs, luminescent metal–organic frameworks (LMOFs) are by far the most widely explored type of MOFs sensor to date. On the one hand, the sustainable pores in MOFs can offer a natural habitat for guest molecules to improve the possibility for guest–host interaction, thus the sensing sensitivity would be improved [19]. On the other hand, the non-radiative relaxation of the organic ligands fixed in the rigid framework can potentially be reduced, thus resulting in stronger fluorescence (FL) emission [20]. Also, the rigid framework can endow MOFs with high thermal stability and the crystal structure can sustain temperatures of hundreds of degrees [21], providing structural support for fluorescent guests even in high temperature settings. Those features promise luminescent frameworks distinct advantages over other candidate classes of sensory materials. And rational design and build-up of these architectures become the primary tasks in exploration of MOFs as sensing material.

Luckily, introduction of guest luminescent nanoparticles with high luminous quantum yield (QY) into the non-luminescent MOFs lights an ideal method for LMOFs fabrication for chemical sensing. It is recently reported that the LMOFs with excellent luminescence property would be favorably obtained by encapsulating guest molecules in the pores of MOFs [22,23]. And in our recent report, by conjugating aggregation-induced emission luminogens (AIEgens) with ZIF-8, a novel kind of LMOFs has been facilely synthesized for ultrasensitive copper ion sensing and visual glucose detection [8]. Besides, as an emerging nano-carbon material [24], CDs have stimulated researchers’ extremely great interest and been widely used in manufacturing a variety of fluorescent probes and sensors due to their excellent properties including high photoluminescence stability [25], environmental friendliness [25], easy preparation [26,27], low toxicity [28] as well as excellent biocompatibility [29–31] and water solubility [32,33]. Meanwhile, among various types of MOFs, ZIF-8 is an ideal substrate of preparing functional composites due to its unique advantages such as porous appearance, large pores, high stability, easy synthesis, and benign nature [34,35]. Their porous substrate can accommodate smaller nanoparticles, resulting in the composites with better functions. We are thus inspired to attempt to prepare and synthesize LMOFs in a simple but effective way by adopting CDs with high QY as guest fluorescent nanoparticles.

Thanks to the excellent properties of CDs and ZIF-8, by embedding CDs into ZIF-8 crystal at room temperature, the composites CDs@ZIF-8 were fabricated via a simple one-pot method, and employed to build a LMOFs sensor for sensitive and selective detection of dopamine (DA). DA is a catecholamine neurotransmitter of human central nervous system in the brain, and participates in many biological processes in hormonal, central nervous and cardiovascular systems [36,37]. The relative lack of DA might lead to lose control of muscle movement and result in distraction and even Parkinson's disease. Besides, the abnormal concentration of DA could be a potential signal of several diseases. Thus, accurate determination and quantification of DA is a significant and challenging issue. Up to now, numerous methods including electrochemical analysis [38,39], ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer [40], high performance liquid chromatography [41] and fluorescent probes [42,43] have been developed to determine DA. Nevertheless, some limitations of the reported methods, such as burdensome instrument procedure, intricate sample pre-treatment process, toxic substances and organic solvent would restrict their applications. To conquer these shortcomings, it is highly desirable to develop facile, eco-friendly, non-toxic and economic methods to determine DA. And in this work, by virtue of the synergistic effect of CDs and ZIF-8, the analyte molecules DA can be pre-concentrated in the sustainable pores within the ZIF-8 frame and independently oxidized to its produce dopamine-quinone under the action of the basic site on ZIF-8. Then the dopamine-quinone can be absorbed on the ZIF-8 framework by π-π interaction with the aromatic ring of ZIF-8. Besides, the electrostatic interaction between dopamine-quinone and CDs would cause FL quenching of CDs, thus a novel detection method is developed by virtue of synergy of properties of ZIF-8 and CDs. In addition, the as-prepared LMOFs (CDs@ZIF-8) not only exhibit an outstanding FL activity but also can selectively concentrate DA to afford sensitive detection of DA. Besides, the as-developed FL sensor showed high specificity and selectivity when we challenged the detection by using potential interferents or in complicated serum and urine.

The strategy that thermal treatment of molecular organic salts was adopted to prepare CDs [44]. In the preparation procedure, glutathione (GSH, 153 mg) and citric acid (CA) monohydrate (105 mg) were dissolved in 2 mL of water at flask and ultrasound to make its dispersed, and then dried in a vacuum for 12 h at 70 °C. The evaporated thick syrup was heated in oil bath for 10 min at 200 °C. The brown syrup product was then diluted to 2 mL using ultrapure water. After full reaction, the product was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 15 min to remove the non-fluorescent precipitate. Subsequently, the supernatant was taken out and neutralized with 1 M NaOH, and then filtered with a filter membrane (0.22 μm) to dislodge the large particles product. Finally, the fluorescent CDs were gathered by dialysing against deionized water for 24 h via a dialysis membrane (1000 MWCO), and the as-prepared CDs solution was stored in refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

Firstly, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (2.55 mM, 40 mL methanol solution) and Hmin (7.65 mM, 40 mL methanol solution) were mixed with 400 μL of CDs and agitated overnight on a magnetic stirrer at room temperature. Until the mixture solution has been transformed to turbid canary yellow milky dispersion, kept it for 30 min, and the final product was obtained by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for another 5 min, washed three times with methanol, and then dried in vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h. As a control, ZIF-8 crystals without CDs were synthesized in the same procedure by adding no CDs.

The detection of DA was performed in phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 10 mM, pH = 7.0). In a typical assay, the CDs@ZIF-8 powder (1 mg) was dispersed absolutely in 4 mL of DA with different concentrations ranged from 0 μM to 200 μM at room temperature. Under the action of the active site on the ZIF-8 framework, DA would be polymerized to form dopamine-quinone, and the interaction between dopamine-quinone and CDs would result in the FL quenching of CDs. The FL data was measured after one hour. The selectivity for DA was evaluated by adding Na+, K+, Mg2+ or Arginine, Lysine, Leucine, Tyrosine, Serine, Threonine, Noradrenaline, Sucrose, Glucose, N-Acetylsulfanilyl chloride, Uric acid and Glutathione (Arg, Lys, Leu, Tyr, Ser, Thr, NE, Suc, Glu, Asc, UA and GSH) instead of DA in a same route at a concentration of 200 μM. All the FL measurement was conducted on a PerkinElmer LS 55 spectrophotometer equipped with a 1 × 1 cm2 quartz cell under the given conditions including that the excitation wavelength of 347 nm, the emission and excitation emission slit widths of 5 nm, and photomultiplier tube voltage of automatic voltage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in human serum and urine
ce:section.ce:para: The practicality and feasibility of the developed CDs@ZIF-8-based sensor were performed by generalizing the detection to human serum and urine. The human serum and urine were obtained from The Third XiangYa Hospital of Central South University, and this study was approved by the Medical Ethics Committee of The Third Xiangya Hospital of Central South University. These samples were diluted by 10-fold with PBS (10 mM, pH = 7.0) before to be used. Then, diluted serum and urine samples (4 mL) were added into CDs@ZIF-8 with DA standard solutions (final concentration of 0.6, 6 and 10 μM respectively). The FL signal intensity was recorded at 432 nm (excited at 347 nm) after 1 h.

As the synthesis schematic diagram of CDs@ZIF-8 shown in Fig. 1 , we firstly synthesized the blue fluorescent CDs by referring to the previous report [44], and its morphology was confirmed by TEM. As the results shown in Fig. S1, the CDs feature clearly revealed their spherical morphology and the average diameter was 2.6 nm. In addition, the FL QY of CDs was determined using quinine sulphate as the standard (measured at 350 nm excitation wavelength, QY = 54%), the average QY of CDs in aqueous solution at room temperature was 73% (>40%), which is relatively high among analogous CDs [31,45–48]. It can be seen from the above characterization results that the prepared CDs have small size and high QY.

Then, by directly adding the obtained CDs into the mother liquor of ZIF-8 crystals, we prepared the CDs@ZIF-8 composites via an in-situ assembly strategy. To obtain the optimal FL intensity, the stock solution of CDs in different doses (100 μL, 200 μL, 400 μL, 800 μL and 1600 μL) was added into the mother liquor of ZIF-8 respectively. As the results shown in Fig. S2A, the FL intensity of prepared CDs@ZIF-8 composites would not increase over 400 μL of CDs stock solution, which might be attribute to that 400 μL of CDs would saturate in the pores of ZIF-8. And with the excitation wavelength at 347 nm, the normalized FL emission spectra of the obtained CDs@ZIF-8 almost the same as that of CDs with the maximum FL emission at 432 nm (Fig. S2B). However, no obvious emission peak can be observed for original ZIF-8 at 432 nm, which indicates that the blue FL of CDs@ZIF-8 comes from CDs instead of ZIF-8 crystals, and the FL property of the CDs embedded in ZIF-8 remains unchanged.

We then characterized the resulted CDs@ZIF-8 by different methods to confirm the successful synthesis of the CDs@ZIF-8 composites. As the TEM results shown in Fig. S2C, the prepared CDs@ZIF-8 composites exhibit a uniform diamond-shaped morphology with an average particle size of approximate 200 nm. The FTIR spectrum of CDs (Fig. S2D) demonstrates the absorptions of 3432 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1, which would be attributed to N–H and C = O stretching vibrations respectively. Besides, the peak at 1423 cm−1 could be associated with C–H, N–H. The bands of C-O bending vibrations appeared at 1128 cm−1. The characteristic peak of ZIF-8 at 3432 cm−1 was corresponded to N–H stretching, while the absorption at 2929 cm−1 reflected the C–H stretching vibrations. The absorption at 1629 cm−1 could be assigned to C = C stretching, 1421 cm−1 and 1452 cm−1 were the bending vibrations of N–H. The peaks from 1307 cm−1 to 955 cm−1 could be ascribed to C-N stretching vibrations. Two peaks at 755 cm−1 and 690 cm−1 were N–H waging vibrations. After the CDs have been embedded to the pores of ZIF-8, characteristic peaks corresponding to carboxylic groups and C-H2 groups of CDs were all disappeared, and the FTIR spectrum of CDs@ZIF-8 composites revealed a lot of characteristic absorption peaks similar to that of pure ZIF-8 crystals. These observations were consistent with the results of XRD, indicating the successful assembly of the CDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposite. Besides, the FL stability of CDs@ZIF-8 was also verified (Fig. S3) that the resulted FL intensity did not change significantly under natural light for 30 days, demonstrating that the CDs@ZIF-8 owns excellent stability against photobleaching. Meanwhile, the crystal structures of synthesized CDs@ZIF-8 and original ZIF-8 were also characterized through XRD analysis. The diffraction peak at 2θ = 7.3° is attributed to peak 110 (Fig. S2E), which is consistent with the finding that the crystal structure of ZIF-8 is diamond-shaped and composed of 12 faces [49]. And similar diffraction peaks were also observed in the XRD diffraction spectra of pure ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8, indicating that the integrated crystal structure of ZIF-8 was not destroyed by doping CDs. However, it is notable that the XRD characteristic diffraction peaks of the CDs particles cannot be observed in the XRD diffraction spectrum of CDs@ZIF-8, which is possibly due to that the peak of small CDs is too weak to be clearly observed in the diffraction spectrum of large ZIF-8 crystals.

The thermal stability of ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 was also confirmed by TGA analysis in N2 flow. According to the Fig. S2F, the CDs@ZIF-8 composites are initially decomposed at 175 °C but tend to be stable at around 500 °C with a weight loss of about 12%, which is much lower than that of the pure ZIF-8. The weight loss before 500 °C may be attributed to the volatilization of residual solvents contained in the material and decomposition of the amide groups in CDs. When the temperature is higher than 500 °C, the weight loss of CDs@ZIF-8 composites is possibly caused by the breakdown of ZIF-8 frame. Besides, the weight loss between the temperature 500 °C and 605 °C is only around 21%, indicating that both CDs@ZIF-8 and ZIF-8 exhibit excellent thermal stability. Finally, the BET characterization of CDs@ZIF-8 was conducted. As the result shown in Fig. S4, CDs@ZIF-8 exhibits typical Type-I BET isotherm and microporous characteristics, and its specific surface area is calculated as 793.94 m2 g−1 based on the adsorption measurement of N2 by isotherm. Compared with pure ZIF-8 (1142.80 m2 g−1), the specific surface area of BET for CDs@ZIF-8 is decreased as non-porous CDs are incorporated into ZIF-8. Additionally, the distributions of pore size for ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 are the same, indicating that embedded CDs are surrounded by the ZIF-8 framework, which might be attributed to that the large sizes of CDs do not permit them to occupy cavities of the ZIF-8.

The imidazole ring of ZIF-8 has been proved possessing a high binding affinity to compounds with conjugated π bond via π-π stacking [50]. Thus, as a proof of concept, the interaction between CDs@ZIF-8 composites and analyte DA was investigated. The mixed solution of CDs@ZIF-8 and DA solution revealed an evident milky white color (Fig. 2 A), but the color of the mixed solution altered from milky white to black (Fig. 2B) after a while, which could be ascribed to the formation of dopamine-quinone and the subsequent adsorption of dopamine-quinone on the surface of ZIF-8. Besides, as shown in Fig. 2C and D, the strong blue emission of CDs@ZIF-8 also vanished after the addition of DA (100 μM).

We then want to explore the FL quenching mechanism of CDs@ZIF-8 by DA. And as is well known, dopamine can be oxidized by ambient O2 to generate dopamine-quinone species in alkaline solution. It was reported that the product dopamine-quinone species exhibit a characteristic absorption around 390 nm [51]. In this work, we also demonstrated the generation of dopamine-quinone species by recording the UV–vis spectra before and after the incubation of DA under basic condition (Fig. S5). Previous reports have shown that dopamine-quinone is an electron acceptor for QDs [8,37,52,53]. Therefore, as the possible mechanism for the detection of DA by CDs shown in Fig. 3 A: the CD solution exhibited strong fluorescence intensity (FL) in the absence of DA, and the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of the CDs enable noncovalent interaction with diols, amine functional groups, and phenyl of DA through electrostatic interactions, π-π stacking, and hydrogen bonding. Under alkaline conditions, DA is oxidized to dopamine-quinone that can accept electrons from the photoexcited CDs with consequent fluorescence quenching. The time-resolved fluorescence spectra of the CDs@ZIF-8 in the absence and presence of DA were measured to further investigate the quenching mechanism. As the fluorescence decay curves shown in Fig. 3B and the fluorescence lifetimes listed in Table S1, the fluorescence lifetimes of the CDs@ZIF-8 were almost the same in the absence and presence of DA, indicating that the quenching mechanism involves static quenching. In addition, As shown in Fig. 3C, dopamine-quinone is tightly adsorbed on the surface of CDs@ZIF-8, which would benefit the interaction between dopamine-quinone and CDs, and thus quenching the FL emission of CDs via electrostatic interaction.

After verifying the FL quenching of CDs@ZIF-8 by DA, an FL sensing system was developed for DA detection by adopting the CDs@ZIF-8 as the probe. And the selectivity of the CDs@ZIF-8 based FL sensing system was firstly evaluated. Several potential interferents (200 μM) including 6 kinds of amino acids (Arg, Lys, Leu, Tyr, Ser and Thr), Na+, K+, Mg2+, NE, Suc, Glu Asc, UA, and GSH were introduced to investigate their FL response for CDs@ZIF-8 and compared with that of the DA. As the results shown in Fig. S6, except DA, other compounds scarcely caused FL changes of CDs@ZIF-8, indicating that CDs@ZIF-8 has high selectivity for DA detection. The prominent performance could be attributed to the enrichment and selective capture of DA by the basic site on ZIF-8. Besides, the FL signal intensity of the CDs@ZIF-8 is quenched monotonically with the increasing concentration of DA in a wide concentration from 0.1 to 200 μM (Fig. 4 ), and the relationship can be quantified by a non-linear correlation equation:
F 0
/ F = 0.026 ×
e
x / 0.97
+ 1.0169

To evaluate the practical application of the CDs@ZIF-8 based sensor, standard addition experiments were conducted in human serum and urine to verify the practicability of the detection. As the results shown in Table 2 , the relative standard deviations (RSDs) of the samples are also less than 0.22, and the recovery rates are between 92.67% and 97.30%, demonstrating that the CDs@ZIF-8 fluorescent probe can be potentially used to detect DA in the human serum and urine.

In summary, by introducing a simple one-pot method, the CDs@ZIF-8 composites have been facially fabricated via an in-situ embedding strategy. Based on the as-prepared CDs@ZIF-8 composites, a simple and cost-effective DA detection method has been favorably developed by employing ZIF-8 as the target recognition unit while CDs in the pores of ZIF-8 frame as the signal transduction unit. The as-prepared CDs@ZIF-8 composites not only have uniform size, but also demonstrate excellent FL efficiency. More importantly, the CDs@ZIF-8 composites possess good thermal stability and FL stability, and demonstrate high selectivity, sensitivity and anti-interference performance for DA detection. This work reported a new strategy of integrating advantages of fluorescent nanoparticles and MOFs to develop novel FL composites for sensing applications, which would provide a promising pathway to construct various functional sensors based on nanoparticles@ZIFs or MOFs hybrid materials.

Siqi Xie: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Xujie Li: Validation, Data curation. Lumin Wang: Software, Validation. Fawei Zhu: Visualization, Investigation. Xinyi Zhao: Validation. Tianqi Yuan: Data curation. Qi Liu: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Xiaoqing Chen: Resources, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21878339, 21904141 and 22078369), the Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 2019JJ50791), the Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Safety Monitoring and Early Warning (No. 2020KFJJ06), and the Key R&D Project in the industrial field funded by Hunan Provincial Science &Technology Department, China (2019GK2131).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105718.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

C 8 (2020) 10408-10415.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoluminescent metal-organic frameworks for gas sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Lin, S. Liu, J. Ye, X. Li, J. Zhang, Photoluminescent metal-organic frameworks for gas sensing, Adv. Sc. 3 (2016) 1500434.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of a monolithic magnetic stir bar for the determination of sulfonylurea herbicides coupled with HPLC

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile fabrication of magnetic metal-organic framework nanocomposites for potential targeted drug delivery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.K. Prasad, D.H. Hong, M.P. Suh, High gas sorption and metal-ion exchange of microporous metal-organic frameworks with incorporated imide groups, Chem-Eur J. 16 (2010) 14043-14050.
